mnemonics: HILL: Have Invertible Linear Layout (for the key matrix to work for decryption)
K.I.S.S. for Hill: Keep It Square & Singular-free (the key matrix must be square and have an inverse modulo 26)
Detective Hill: A matrix detective needs a mod 26 inverse to 'decrypt' the case
keyPoints: Hill Cipher is a polygraphic substitution cipher based on linear algebra, encrypting blocks of letters at a time.
The core operation is matrix multiplication modulo 26: Ciphertext = (Key Matrix * Plaintext Vector) mod 26.
Decryption requires the key matrix to be invertible modulo 26; the decryption key is the inverse of this matrix mod 26.
commonMistakes: Using a key matrix that is not invertible modulo 26 (its determinant is 0 or shares a factor with 26), which makes decryption impossible.
Misaligning plaintext into vectors (e.g., incorrect padding for incomplete blocks) or errors in matrix arithmetic modulo 26.
Confusing the encryption and decryption processes, particularly using the original key matrix instead of its inverse for decryption.
